tasks.title: Cash Register Change
tasks.description:
When you compute change after a purchase, several arithmetic operations often happen in sequence: subtraction, division, and remainder. These calculations are a simple way to practice working with values stored in variables and combining operators carefully.

Read two integers from stdin: the amount paid and the cost, both in cents. Assume the amount paid is greater than or equal to the cost and both are between 0 and 1,000,000 inclusive. Compute the change in cents, then print how many whole dollars and how many leftover cents are in that change, separated by one space.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 500 237 → change: 263 → dollars: 2 → cents: 63. Output: 2 63

tasks.title: Average With Remainder
tasks.description:
Many measurements are stored as whole numbers, but it is often useful to split a result into a quotient and a remainder. This appears in time calculations, packaging, and simple data summaries where exact averages are not whole numbers.

Read three integers from stdin: a, b, and c. Each value is between -1,000,000 and 1,000,000 inclusive. Compute the sum of the three numbers, then print the integer part of the average followed by the remainder when the sum is divided by 3, separated by one space. Use C integer division rules, and assume the sum fits in a 32-bit signed integer.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 10 11 13 → sum: 10 → 21 → 34 → average: 11 → remainder: 1. Output: 11 1

tasks.title: Mixed Unit Distance
tasks.description:
Distances are often written using different units, and converting them into a single compact form requires careful use of division and remainder. This kind of calculation is common when working with seconds, minutes, meters, centimeters, or any other base-and-subunit system.

Read one integer from stdin representing a total distance in centimeters. The value will be between 0 and 1,000,000 inclusive. Compute how many whole meters it contains and how many centimeters remain. Print the result as two integers separated by one space.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 347 → meters: 3 → centimeters: 47. Output: 3 47

tasks.title: Final Score After Penalties
tasks.description:
Scores in games and contests are often adjusted by adding bonuses and subtracting penalties. Working through a few updates in sequence is a good way to practice how expressions combine across multiple statements.

Read three integers from stdin: the starting score, a bonus, and a penalty. Each value is between 0 and 1,000,000 inclusive. Compute the final score as starting score plus bonus minus penalty, then print the result as a single integer. The final score may be zero or negative.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 80 15 22 → score: 80 → 95 → 73. Output: 73

tasks.title: Recipe Scaling
tasks.description:
Cooking and laboratory work both use proportional changes in quantities. When you scale several ingredients, you must apply multiplication, addition, and subtraction carefully so the final amount is correct.

Read three integers from stdin: a base amount, a scale factor, and an adjustment. Each value is between -100000 and 100000 inclusive. Compute the result of multiplying the base amount by the scale factor and then subtracting the adjustment. Print the final integer value. Assume the intermediate result fits in a 32-bit signed integer.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 12 3 7 → amount: 12 → 36 → 29. Output: 29

tasks.title: Time Split Into Hours and Minutes
tasks.description:
Time values are often stored as a total number of minutes and then converted into a more readable format. This uses both division and remainder, which are fundamental tools for breaking one number into larger and smaller units.

Read one integer from stdin representing a duration in minutes. The value will be between 0 and 1,000,000 inclusive. Print how many whole hours are contained in the duration and how many minutes remain, separated by one space.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 185 → hours: 1 → minutes: 45. Output: 3 25

tasks.title: Simple Tax and Discount
tasks.description:
Prices in stores are often adjusted by applying a percentage and then subtracting a fixed amount. This kind of calculation is a practical use of multiplication, integer division, and several variables in one expression chain.

Read two integers from stdin: a price in cents and a tax rate in percent. The price is between 0 and 1,000,000 inclusive, and the tax rate is between 0 and 100 inclusive. Compute the final price after adding tax and then subtracting 50 cents as a fixed discount. Use integer arithmetic, so any fractional cents from the tax are discarded by C rules. Print the final price in cents as a single integer.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 200 10 → tax: 20 → total: 220 → final: 170. Output: 170

tasks.title: Coordinate Shift
tasks.description:
In graphics and mapping, positions are often updated by adding movement in one direction and subtracting movement in another. Keeping track of these changes with variables helps build intuition for how values are transformed step by step.

Read four integers from stdin: x, y, dx, and dy. Each value is between -100000 and 100000 inclusive. Compute the new position after moving x by dx and y by dy. Print the updated x and y coordinates separated by one space.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 10 20 -3 7 → x: 10 → 7, y: 20 → 27. Output: 7 27

tasks.title: Packets and Leftovers
tasks.description:
Packaging problems often ask how many full boxes can be filled and how many items are left over. This is a classic case for quotient and remainder calculations using integer division.

Read two integers from stdin: the number of items and the box capacity. Both values are between 1 and 1,000,000 inclusive. Compute how many full boxes can be made and how many items remain unpacked. Print the two results separated by one space.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 53 8 → boxes: 6 → leftovers: 5. Output: 6 5

tasks.title: Temperature Shift in Two Steps
tasks.description:
Measurements are frequently adjusted by first applying one change and then another. Doing the updates in order is useful when a value is corrected, calibrated, or converted between systems.

Read three integers from stdin: a starting value, a first adjustment, and a second adjustment. Each integer is between -100000 and 100000 inclusive. First add the first adjustment to the starting value, then subtract the second adjustment from that result. Print the final value as a single integer.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 15 8 4 → value: 15 → 23 → 19. Output: 19
